{{Short description|Radioactive decay process in which a beta particle is emitted from an atomic nucleus}}
{{Infobox physics
| name = Beta decay
| image = Beta-minus Decay.svg
| caption = In β⁻ decay, a [[neutron]] transforms into a [[proton]], emitting an [[electron]] and an [[antineutrino]].
| topics = [[Nuclear physics]], [[Weak interaction]], [[Neutrino]], [[Radioactive decay]]
}}

In [[nuclear physics]], '''beta decay''' (β-decay) is a type of [[radioactive decay]] in which a [[beta particle]] (fast, energetic [[electron]] or [[positron]]) is emitted from an [[atomic nucleus]]. Beta decay is mediated by the [[weak interaction]] and occurs in two main forms: β⁻ and β⁺ decay.

== Types ==

=== Beta-minus decay (β⁻) ===
A [[neutron]] in the nucleus converts into a [[proton]], emitting an [[electron]] and an electron [[antineutrino]]:
:<math>n \rightarrow p + e^- + \bar{\nu}_e</math>

This increases the [[atomic number]] by 1 and produces a [[beta particle]] (the electron). Example: [[Carbon-14]] decay.
:<math>^{14}_6\text{C} \rightarrow ^{14}_7\text{N} + e^- + \bar{\nu}_e</math>

=== Beta-plus decay (β⁺) ===
A [[proton]] converts into a neutron, emitting a [[positron]] and an electron [[neutrino]]:
:<math>p \rightarrow n + e^+ + \nu_e</math>

This decreases the atomic number by 1. Example: [[Sodium-22]] decay.
:<math>^{22}_{11}\text{Na} \rightarrow ^{22}_{10}\text{Ne} + e^+ + \nu_e</math>

=== Electron capture ===
An inner-shell [[electron]] is captured by a proton in the nucleus:
:<math>p + e^- \rightarrow n + \nu_e</math>

== Neutrino hypothesis ==
The continuous energy spectrum of beta decay electrons puzzled physicists in the 1920s. To preserve [[conservation of energy]], [[Wolfgang Pauli]] proposed the existence of the [[neutrino]] in 1930. [[Enrico Fermi]] incorporated this into his theory of beta decay (1933), introducing the [[weak interaction]].

== Selection rules ==
Beta decay obeys [[selection rules]]:
* '''Allowed transitions''': no change in orbital angular momentum (<math>\Delta\ell = 0</math>), spin change <math>\Delta S = 0, \pm 1</math>
* '''Fermi transitions''': electron and neutrino spins anti-aligned (<math>S = 0</math>)
* '''Gamow–Teller transitions''': electron and neutrino spins aligned (<math>S = 1</math>)
* '''Forbidden transitions''': <math>\Delta\ell > 0</math> — much longer half-lives

== Applications ==
* [[Radiocarbon dating]] — determining age of organic materials using <sup>14</sup>C decay
* [[Neutrino]] physics — detection of neutrinos from beta decay
* [[Radiotherapy]] — beta emitters for cancer treatment
* [[Nuclear medicine]] — PET scans using β⁺ emitters
* [[Radiotracer]]s — tracking biochemical processes

== See also ==
{{Portal|Physics}}
* [[Electron]]
* [[Neutrino]]
* [[Neutron]]
* [[Nuclear physics]]
* [[Proton]]
* [[Radioactive decay]]
* [[Weak interaction]]

== References ==
{{Reflist|30em}}

[[Category:Beta decay| ]]
[[Category:Nuclear physics]]
[[Category:Radioactivity]]
[[Category:Weak interaction]]
